summary:
Nader Shah's Invasion of India (1738–1739 AD) was a catastrophic raid by the Persian conqueror who had displaced the [[event:2338|Safavid]] and founded the [[event:3238|Afsharid]] dynasty, demonstrating the total military impotence of the [[event:2446|declining Mughal Empire]] and stripping Delhi of treasures accumulated over two centuries of Mughal rule. Nader Shah, one of the most capable military commanders of the 18th century — who had reconquered Persia from Afghan invaders, expelled the Ottomans from western Persia, and reconstituted an empire stretching from the Caucasus to the Oxus — invaded India on the pretext of Mughal failure to prevent border raids into Persia. The Mughal army met him at the [[event:2380|Battle of Karnal]] (February 1739) and was decisively destroyed; the Mughal Emperor Muhammad Shah was captured.

Nader Shah entered Delhi in March 1739 and his troops were there for weeks. When a rumor spread that he had been killed and Delhi's population attacked his soldiers, he ordered a general massacre — the Qatl-e-Am — in which an estimated 30,000 Delhi residents were killed in a single day. The looting that followed was immense: the Peacock Throne (estimated value equivalent to many years of Mughal revenues), the Koh-i-Noor diamond, the Timur Ruby, and enormous quantities of jewelry, gold, and jeweled articles were carried off to Persia. Contemporary estimates placed the total value of the plunder at 700 million rupees — more than a year of Mughal tax revenues. Nader Shah withdrew but retained the Punjab territories west of the Indus; the psychological and fiscal damage to the Mughal state was permanent. Delhi never fully recovered; the event confirmed to the regional governors that the empire they nominally served could not protect them, accelerating [[event:2446|centrifugal disintegration]].

read_more_links.title: Peacock Throne Koh-i-Noor looted Delhi Persia
